texts.orig: Permeability functions are developed for pure  gases and  mixtures of  N2, CH4, CO2, and  02  as  a  function of  driving pressure for polysulfone and  silicone rubber membranes.  The  gas  mixtures used  in the  measurements  include N2-O2, CO2-N2-O2, and CH4-N2--CO2. The functions are  expressed in  a  linear form  in  terms  of  species partial pressures. The functions are  in  good agreement  with  the  measured  data. Positive and  large weights  for the  fastest permeating species, CO2, are  obtained in  the permeability functions for  all  species  in  polysulfone. The opposite is  obtained for  the  slower  permeating  species in  the polysulfone permeability functions. This  result is  consistent with  the  membrane  behaviour, where permeability of  faster species is  reduced  by presence of  slower  species. The species weights  in  the  silicone rubber permeability functions are  1-5 orders  of  magnitudes  smaller  than  the  function constant. This behaviour  is  dictated by limited variations in species permeability in  silicone rubber.

texts.orig: In this  study, permeability functions are  obtained for  N2,  02,  CO2, and  CH4  in  polysulfone and silicone rubber membranes. Both membranes  are commercially available and are  provided  from manufacturers in  the  form  of  fiat sheets or  hollow  fibers. The developed  functions are  obtained by  analysis of  data  sets for various  gas mixtures  as well as pure gases.  The functions are expressed  in terms of species  partial pressure  in  the  feed  side  of  the  permeator.  This is motivated by previous literature studies on separation properties for  pure  and  mixture gases  in  various types

texts.orig: Several  investigators analysed  the conditioning effects of  CO2  on  glassy polymers  [1-5]. The analysis by Chiou and Paul [l], studied  the  effect of CO2 conditioning  in  polycarbonate  and  poly(methyl methacrylate). Their  results show  different performances  for  the two membranes  as a function  of CO2  conditioning and  driving pressure. For  the  polycarbonate membranes,  permeability for pure  CO2  and CH 4 are reduced  upon conditioning with CO2  and increase of  the  driving pressure. On the  other hand,  the permeability for  the  two gases  in  the  acrylic membrane  is found  to  increase upon  CO2  conditioning and the  increase of  the  driving pressure. Increase in  pure gas permeability in  the  acrylic membranes is  attributed  to  high  levels of  CO2  plasticization and subsequently  increased  rates  of segmental motion of polymer  chains. This  behaviour is the  opposite of  that found  in  the  polycarbonate membranes.  In  this regard, it  is  assumed that  exposure  of the polycarbonate membranes  to  carbon  dioxide  alters the  segmental organisation, which does  not  recover to  its original state upon removal  of  CO2. These  findings are  also reported in  similar studies for  CO2  in  poly(ethylene terephthalate) [2], CO2, N2, and Ar in  polycarbonate [3,4], and CH4 and CO2  in  polycarbonate films  [5].

texts.orig: The  previous  conclusion for acrylic films  also applies to  cellulose acetate membranes [6-11 ]. Transport  properties of  pure  gases, CO2, N2, and CH4  are tested for both  dense  homogenous (symmetric) membranes  as  well  as asymmetric  membranes. In  these studies analysis is  performed to  prove  that cellulose acetate membranes  behave in a similar manner to other  glassy membranes. This  conclusion is  arrived at  through  analysis of  species solubility, diffusivity, and  permeability as  a  function of  temperature, membrane  thickness, and driving pressure. Results show that  increase  in the permeability of pure gases occurred upon the  increase of  the  driving pressure. This  behaviour is  attributed to  membrane plasticiza- tion by CO2. Further, in  binary mixtures of  CO2-N2 and CO2-CH4, lower  permeability is  measured for CO2  than  those measured  for the  pure  CO2  gas. Also, higher permeability is  measured  for the  slower  species, N2 and CH4, than  those for pure  N2 or  CH 4 gases. In  this regard, competition among permeating species would result in  lower  CO2  permeability. On the  other hand,  membrane  plasticization would enhance  permeation  of  the  slower  species.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Effect of  polymer  structure on permeation characteristics is investigated by Stern  et al.  [12].  The investigation included development  of  nine  types  of polyimide  membranes.  Permeability results show improved selectivity for  polyimide  versus  conventional glassy polymers, which  include cellulose acetate, polysulfone, and  polycarbonate. This  behaviour is attributed to  chain  stiffness and  packing  density. The authors  [12]  have concluded  that  calibration of  the packing  density as  a function of  properties of  penetrant molecules  is  necessary to  achieve the  required permeation rates. Permeability of CH4, He,  and  CO2 were  measured  in  three types of  thermoplastics [13], which  include  polyethersulfone, polysulfone, and polycarbonate. Membrane  selectivity for  He/CH4  is measured  at  50,  61,  and 80 for  polycarbonate, polysulfone, and polyethersulfone, respectively. On  the other hand,  variations in  the  selectivity of CO2/CH4 are  reported at  smaller and  lower range  of  23-28.  The lowest  membrane  permeability is  measured  for  the polyethersulfone membranes. This is attributed to more efficient packing  of  the  polymer  chain. Similar findings and  conclusions are  reported in  the  study  by Kamps et  al. [14]. The study included nitration effects of polysulfone  and  polyethersulfone membranes. Nitration effects of  both  the  polymers  are  found  to reduce membrane permeability and  increase the  selectivity of CO2]CH4.

texts.orig: by Seok et al.  [16] and Sengupta et al.  [17] are motivated  by properties of conventional separation systems, which include  liquid-liquid extraction and distillation. I these systems, hybrid arrangements and the  use of more than  one solvent are  practised to achieve  the  desired  separation level. The study  by Seok  et  al. [16]  focused on  characterisation of  separation properties of  He,  CH4, C2H4, and  C2H6  by  using two different membranes.  The measurements  are  performed  in  a configuration of  two-membrane column, which includes silicone rubber  and cellulose acetate membranes.  This  selection is  made to  obtain a  highly concentrated product (99% He).  In  this configuration, variation in  the  average of  the  separation factor for He in  cellulose acetate with  respect to  the  hydrocarbons increases from  6  in  the  pure  state to  a  higher value  of 19  in the  gas  mixture. Li  [18] and  Henis  et al. [19]  used a  different approach, where  composite membranes are formed  of  porous  ceramic material covered with  a  thin polymer  layer. Their  work is  motivated by the  search for  cheaper  and selective separation systems.

texts.orig: Species  permeability in commercial type membranes, which includes silicone rubber  and cellulose acetate, were measured for  pure  and mixture  gases [20,21]. The study  by Hughes et  al.  [20]  examined possibilities of  separation of  nitrous oxide  from  mixtures with  02 and CO2  by using  cellulose acetate or silicone rubber  membranes. Results  show N20/CO2 membrane selectivity close unity  in  cellulose acetate and  around  1.3  for silicone rubber. System  operation at various temperatures  or  feed pressures  did  not improve  the  separation characteristics for either membrane.  The study  by Ettouney  et  al.  [21]  considered separation properties of  mixtures of CO2-N2 in  silicone rubber  and cellulose acetate membranes. The results show that  the  pure  gas  permeability is  independent  of  the  system  pressure. This  behaviour is  also observed for  mixtures with  lean  or  rich gas  compositions, i.e., 90%  CO2  in  N2. Permeability dependence on  the  system  pressure is  found  in  feed  mixtures with compositions  of 50% CO2 in N  2. Comparison of separation factors for  silicone rubber  and cellulose acetate is  found  to  be similar for  the  mixture  gases. This is  irrespective of the fact  that  the separation factors for  the pure gases  in cellulose acetate are 5-7 times  higher. This  behaviour  was attributed to plasticization effects of  the  CO2  gas  on the  cellulose acetate membrane.

texts.orig: Two separation cells are  used  in  the  measurements. The first is  a hollow-fiber polysulfone cell  and the second  is  a flat-sheet silicone rubber  cell. The polysulfone cells were  provided by PERMEA, USA.  The silicone rubber  flat sheets are  acquired from BibbySterilin, UK.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The polysulfone fibers have  an average inner  diameter  of  0.15  mm  and  an  outer diameter of  0.45  mm. The system  has  a  total separation area of  2.24  m 2.  The system  overall dimensions  are  60  cm  in  length  and 5 cm in  diameter. The polysulfone system  has  a  shell and tube  configuration. The feed  gas is  introduced inside the  fibers and the  permeate  is  collected on the shell  side.  The silicone rubber  membranes  are  flat sheets with  a diameter of 10  cm  and a thickness of 38 ktm. The  net  permeation  area  for  the sheets  is 6x 10  -3  m 2.  Schematics  of  the  polysulfone and silicone  rubber systems  as  well  as  other components  are shown in  Fig.  1.

The digital flow meters  are  originally calibrated for pure  nitrogen at  a pressure of  101.325  kPa and  room temperature. Corrections of  the flow  meter  readings are  determined as  a function of  the  stream  pressure and its composition. The corrections are  obtained for gas  mixtures and  pure gases. Correction factors are  the  correlation results for the  readings of  the flow  meter  and  a  gas  bubble  meter, which measures the flow rate  at the atmospheric pressure.

Correction factors are  the  correlation results for the  readings of  the flow  meter  and  a  gas  bubble  meter, which measures the flow rate  at the atmospheric pressure.

texts.orig: The digital flow  meters  are  used  for  measurements of  the  reject and  permeate  streams in  the  polysulfone system. This  is because the polysulfone membrane has a  large membrane area, which  can  generate high  stage cuts. Accordingly the  permeate  flow  rate  has large values which  are  not  affected by the  meter  accuracy. On the  other hand,  the gas  bubble meter  is used  in  the majority of  measurements  for  the  permeate  flow  in silicone rubber. This  is because of  the  small membrane area  which  results in  much lower  permeate  flow  rates with  values  close to  the  meter  accuracy.

texts.orig: Local  vendors in  Kuwait  provided all gases  used  in the experiments. The  gases  are  provided  in highpressure cylinders,  13  000-28  000  kPa,  and  are equipped  with  pressure regulators. The cylinders contained  either pure  gases  (CO2, CH4, 02, or N2) or mixture gases (CO2,  02,  and  N2), (CH4,  CO2, and  N2), or (02 and N2).  Contents  of  the  gas cylinders are specified to the  manufacturer. Contents  of  the  gas cylinders (either pure  or  mixture) were  analysed upon delivery to  obtain accurate composition analysis.

This  parameter  is controlled by a needle  valve  placed  on the  reject stream. The experiments are  performed  over  a feed pressure range  of  239.2  to  790  kPa,  abs,  and reject flow rate  of  3.33x10 -5,  6.67x10 -5, 1×10  -4, and 1.33 x 10  -4  m3/s.

texts.orig: Summary  for  gas  permeability which are  reported in  the  cited literature is  shown in  Table  1.  The table also includes the  results obtained in  this study for pure gases in polysulfone  and  silicone  rubber.  Data reported for  the  four  gases  in  the  acrylic and polycarbonate  are  lower than  those  of silicone rubber, polysulfone, and  cellulose acetate. Irrespective ofthis, selectivity of the acrylic and polycarbonate membranes  are  similar to  those  of  polysulfone and cellulose acetate  membranes.  The  selectivity for the silicone rubber  is  the  lowest  among various types  of membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Permeability functions are  developed  for 02, N2, CO2, and CH4  in  pure  and  mixture gases  for  silicone rubber  and polysulfone. Measured permeability is fitted against linear functions of  partial pressures of the  gases on  the feed  side. Error analysis for measured data  versus  model predictions in  polysulfone varied from  high  values, above  R2=0.88-0.95. The analysis error for  the  permeability functions in  silicone rubber is  varied over  a  range  of  0.6-0.69.

texts.orig: Measured permeability data  in  polysulfone (glassy polymer)  are consistent with literature studies. In this  regard, presence  of faster permeating  species enhances  permeation  rates of slower  species. This behaviour  is  found for N2 in air  and in the CO2 mixture, where the  permeability of N  2 in  both  mixtures  is  higher  than  the  permeability for  pure N2. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Developed  permeability functions reflect behaviour of  various gases  in  both  membranes.  In  polysulfone membranes,  weights of  slower  permeating species in the  permeability functions of  faster species are  either negative or  have  a  positive and  smaller value than  the coefficient ofthe  faster permeating species. The  opposite result is  found  for  weights  of  faster permeating species  in the permeability functions  of slower species.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Species weights are  1-5  orders of  magnitude  smaller  than the function constant  in all  permeability functions obtained for the  silicone rubber membrane. This  result reflects limited variations in  the  species permeability as  a function of  the  system  pressure or composition.  This result is the opposite  to that obtained for  polysulfone, where the  species weights are  only  1-3  orders of  magnitudes  smaller than  the function constant. The behaviour reflect larger variations in  species permeability as  a  function of  system pressure and composition.
